chapters.title: Freedom anchors Ukrainian thought
chapters.text:
Ukrainian literary education traces back to the 1700s and philosopher Hryhoriy Skovoroda, who wandered eastern Ukraine sharing ideas meant to stir young minds. Students still draw strength at his monument; each year the president and Kyiv-Mohyla Academy students clean the memorial together as a ritual of continuity.

Skovoroda never aimed to build a rigid philosophical system. After graduating from Kyiv-Mohyla Academy he traveled Europe for years, then taught briefly before resigning — his methods clashed with institutional convention and he disagreed with how schools drilled students. He chose an ascetic life of travel, writing fables, dialogues, and treatises instead.

In his poem De Libertate he placed freedom and self-knowledge at the center of a happy life: embrace who you are and the work you were made for.

Skovoroda said gold is a pile of mud compared to freedom.

Meanwhile, near Poltava, Ivan Kotliarevsky — a giant of early modern Ukrainian letters — was studying and working. His masterpiece Eneida retells Virgil's epic in colloquial Ukrainian, parodying the original to expose social problems. Fiery Ukrainian soldiers follow Aeneas through a story that is hilarious, alive, and still resonant centuries later.

Eneida showcases "living" Ukrainian: 7,000 words spanning household objects and folk rituals, packed with idioms Kotliarevsky dared to use when seriousness was the norm.

Monuments to Taras Shevchenko stand in thirty-five countries, yet none match his symbolic power during the 2014 Revolution of Dignity. On the poet's bicentennial, activist Serhiy Nigoyan quoted a fragment from Shevchenko's The Caucasus urging resistance. After Nigoyan's death, those lines became Maidan's rallying cry.

Born a serf, Shevchenko won freedom at twenty-four when artist Karl Bryullov recognized his talent at the St. Petersburg Academy of Arts.

Like Eneida, Shevchenko's verse names social wounds and calls Ukrainians to fight for independence. Kobzar, his landmark collection, pairs plain poetic form with the theme of national liberation.

Eneida and Shevchenko's poetry are similar. Both highlight social issues and call for Ukrainians to fight for independence.

Did you know? Writer Mykola Hohol reportedly disliked Shevchenko's plain style — though some critics suspect envy, since Hohol struggled to find an audience in Ukraine where Shevchenko thrived.

chapters.title: Modernism drew readers to Ukrainian culture
chapters.text:
Western Ukraine produced Ivan Franko, son of a prosperous blacksmith and a noblewoman fallen on hard times. At Lviv University he moved from journalism into politics — activities that earned him four imprisonments by Austrian authorities who feared his influence.

In 1890 Franko co-founded Ukraine's first political party while Lviv remained under Austro-Hungarian rule. Electoral bids failed, and after repeated setbacks he joined the Shevchenko Scientific Society, pouring energy into literature and scholarship instead of ballots.

His output is staggering: 500-plus poems, extensive prose, more than a dozen plays, translations of Shakespeare, Byron, Hugo, Cervantes, Dante, and Mickiewicz, plus major works on literary and cultural theory that still anchor Ukrainian criticism.

By the late nineteenth century Ukrainian writing shifted from heroic uplift toward modernist exploration. Mykhailo Kotsiubynsky became a defining voice in that turn. His prose carries the light, emotion, and landscape of painters like van Gogh and Monet — rendered in words rather than pigment.

Picture escaping a noisy city of deadlines into open country and breathing freely for the first time in weeks. Kotsiubynsky captures that relief in Intermezzo.

In the Hutsul village of Kryvorivnia he immersed himself in local life and folklore, shaping the tragic romance of Ivan and Marichka. That bond with place echoes in Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors and in Vasyl Stefanyk's portraits of ordinary hardship — The Stone Cross, for instance, records peasants' anguish at leaving home forever.

Director Serhiy Paradzhanov filmed Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors as a sensual tapestry of Hutsul song and mountain scenery that introduced the story to global audiences.

Did you know? At Eurovision 2022, Kalush Orchestra frontman Oleh Psiuk wore a traditional Hutsul keptar jacket from the early twentieth century.

chapters.title: Kobylianska and Ukrainka: feminism a century early
chapters.text:
Lesya Ukrainka and Olha Kobylianska — master stylists of Ukrainian letters — often joined fellow writers in mountainous Kryvorivnia. They rested at Ivan Franko's house, debated books, and listened to the Cheremosh River while hunting inspiration. When apart, they wrote letters; Ukrainka loved sparring with Franko, who critiqued her drafts without pulling punches.

Ukrainka inspires generations of Ukrainian women. Raised in a literary household, she devoured books early. Her uncle Mykhailo Drahomanov, a noted critic and philosopher, shaped her worldview and supported her work as folklorist and translator. She began writing at nine, mastered multiple languages, and despite tuberculosis at ten went on to produce a vast body of work that redefined what women could say in print.

Her play The Forest Song enchanted directors and readers with Ukrainian mythology. Enter the woods and Perelesnyk the trickster guides you toward Mavka, whose magic binds you to love forever. You reach the lake, hear the Water Goblin grumble, feel the Mermaid's deadly tickle — all within a love story between Mavka and a mortal man that ends without fairy-tale comfort but opens a rich universe of folk tale.

Kobylianska, slightly older and linked through tender correspondence, championed women's independence — constant learning, self-development, and growth on their own terms. Both women prized freedom, self-expression, mutual support, and professional opportunity above conventional roles.

Their letters read like early documents of feminism and human-rights thinking decades before those terms entered mainstream discourse.

In prose, Kobylianska examined hardship through women's eyes, mapping the moral climate of the intelligentsia and the village's psychological strains with unflinching honesty.

To have such freedom to be your own goal! First, be a goal for yourself, work for your spirit like a bee; enrich it, increase it, and bring it to the point where it becomes radiant, beautiful, exciting, and shining in a thousand colors! ~ Olha Kobylianska

chapters.title: The 1930s: bohemian joy and state terror
chapters.text:
In the late 1920s the Soviet government built a writers' residence in Kharkiv. The Slovo building offered typewriters in every flat, kindergartens, a dining hall, bomb shelter, rooftop solariums, and soundproofing — luxury for authors who usually hid manuscripts from rats in cooking pots and scraped by on editorial wages.

Residents paid off apartments through writer's fees. Ukraine's literary elite became neighbors: public readings, parties, love affairs, betrayals, and celebrations of youth and talent filled the halls with the energy of a campus that also happened to publish national literature.

Mykola Khvylovy was the soul of the group — guitar sessions, songs, debates that became pamphlets. He backed Ukrainization, a cultural movement turning toward Europe and away from Moscow, and formed literary circles around shared aesthetics and political hope.

Khvylovy considered himself a sincere communist who believed an independent Ukrainian culture could thrive inside the USSR. Eventually he saw the gap between revolutionary ideals and Stalinist methods. His short story I am (romance) portrays Bolshevik fanaticism through a hero torn between duty and love for his mother.

Stalin, meanwhile, sought total social control and feared any independent voice.

Historians tie mass deaths under Stalin to paranoia about treason: charges of "ideological treason" and "sabotage" sent thousands to exile or execution without trials that meant anything.

Arrests began a year after writers moved into Slovo. By 1938 more than forty apartment owners were detained; thirty-three were shot, most under forty. This cohort is known as the Executed Renaissance.

In 1933 Khvylovy and Arkady Liubchenko witnessed famine — later called the Holodomor — in Poltava villages. Khvylovy wired the Central Committee; the reply said grain confiscation was proceeding as planned to break peasant resistance.

Soon writer Mykhailo Yalovy was arrested. The next day Khvylovy met friends to discuss saving him, then went to his study. Friends heard a shot. A suicide note lay on the desk.

Yalovy's arrest is the execution of an entire Generation... For what? For us being the most sincere communists? I do not understand anything. ~ Mykola Khvylovy

His death became protest; historians link it to the collapse of national communist hope. The deepest tragedy isn't only the arrests and executions — it's a generation that genuinely believed partial independence was possible inside the Soviet system.

The greatest tragedy of this generation lies not in the multiple arrests and deaths but in the sincere pursuit of an independent Ukraine within the Soviet Union, hoping for partial independence.

The Executed Renaissance teaches a hard twentieth-century lesson: tyrants offer no compromise. Sacrificing freedom means losing everything.

* Learn more about the Slovo building in the summary of A Word About the Writers' Home "Slovo."

chapters.title: Ivan Bahriany and the brand of courage
chapters.text:
By the late 1920s Ivan Bahriany moved through literary circles and published poetry that caught official attention for the wrong reasons. In 1932 authorities arrested him for "counter-revolutionary agitation." Months in solitary confinement and years in exile preceded his novel The Hunters And The Hunted.

The protagonist escapes a train carrying Gulag death-row prisoners. An NKVD major hunts him down, but this freedom-loving hero refuses surrender. By the 1950s the book appeared in English, German, Spanish, and dozens of other languages, spreading a story Soviet censors could not contain.

The Hunters And The Hunted became a worldwide birthday gift for teenagers — a symbol of the fight for justice handed between generations.

Bahriany emigrated to Germany in 1945 and spent his remaining years there. He wrote Why I Am Not Going Back to The Soviet Union, condemning human-rights abuses and Soviet genocide, then Garden of Gethsemane, a novel detailing NKVD torture he endured firsthand.

Did you know? Kyiv bars named Hvylovy and Bahriany serve Ukrainian craft beer, cider, and coffee worth a visit when you're in town.

chapters.title: Against tyranny: the dissident wave
chapters.text:
The Soviet Union lasted until 1991, and resistance ran until the end — yet totalitarian scars remain in memory and institutions. The Sixtiers, the 1960s generation, drove much of Ukraine's dissent after Stalin's shadow began to lift.

After Stalin's death, Nikita Khrushchev's thaw loosened the system's grip enough for literature to breathe again. Sixtiers revived the Executed Renaissance through memorial evenings and new scholarship on Ukrainian modernism, especially by critics Ivan Dziuba, Yevhen Sverstiuk, and Ivan Svitlychny.

The 1960s were a golden age of Ukrainian poetry: sharp political verse replaced softer lyricism, criticizing Soviet rule and Russification with lines audiences memorized. Poets Lina Kostenko, Vasyl Symonenko, Ivan Drach, Mykola Vingranovsky, and Vasyl Stus soon faced official backlash. Some went underground with samizdat — self-published texts passed hand to hand; others compromised and wrote socialist realism to keep publishing.

Socialist realism was the regime's claim to portray reality, only improved. Living inside it felt like Orwell's 1984: praise a distorted world or suffer consequences.

Social realism is a totalitarian utopia.

1965 marked a peak in arrests. That year Paradzhanov's Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors premiered at Kyiv's Ukraina cinema. After the director spoke, Ivan Dziuba addressed the audience about detentions. Journalist Viacheslav Chornovil urged youth to protest repression. Vasyl Stus shouted that censorship silenced truth. Dziuba recalled a siren wailing as internal troops surrounded the hall.

The Sixtiers became human-rights defenders whose work inspired the 1980s generation and writers in independent Ukraine.

The Sixtiers became a generation of human rights defenders. Their activities inspired the future generation of the eighties and those who had already written in independent Ukraine.

Did you know? Paradzhanov's birth surname was Paradzhanyan. In the USSR a national surname could end a career — yet renaming didn't spare him arrests on charges of speculation and "homosexuality."

chapters.title: Carnival as protest
chapters.text:
In 1985 three Lviv poets — Yurii Andrukhovych, Viktor Neborak, and Oleksandr Irvanets — formed Bu-Ba-Bu, launching a festival and literary prize that turned readings into events. Their performances staged carnival, entertained crowds, and built an alternate reality to mock late-Soviet life. Through laughter they processed empire's collapse before analysts had vocabulary for it.

Andrukhovych's first novel Recreations, published toward the end of the 1990s, sends characters resembling Bu-Ba-Bu members through a phantasmagoric carnival. He experimented with language to revive literary tradition after decades of flattening propaganda prose.

His fiction captures post-Soviet chaos and the search for Eastern Europe's identity after the bloc fell apart without a script.

The 1990s in Ukraine mixed uncertainty, hunger for change, and self-mockery as survival skill. Bu-Ba-Bu slammed the door on the past without knowing how painful the corrections ahead would be for ordinary families.

chapters.title: Guard what is yours
chapters.text:
Oksana Zabuzhko, alongside Andrukhovych, helped forge a new literary language and national identity after independence. Born to literary scholars, she trained in philology early and spent years analyzing Ukrainian letters in essays and journalism that argued culture was political, not decorative.

Her prose debut Fieldwork in Ukrainian Sex ignited debate about female sexuality, gender and national identity, and sexual violence in public discourse. The narrator blends poet, intellectual, witch, and harlot — a polyphonic woman searching for herself even within a love plot that refuses simple resolution.

Zabuzhko's sentences stream long and expressive; her fiction stirs emotion yet often reads like critical essay because ideas and feeling arrive together.

By the late 1990s another voice rose: Serhii Zhadan began as a poet blending humor and brutality, then found a signature rhythm audiences recognize instantly. Hear him read once and the melody stays in your body.

His 2004 novel Depeche Mode opened prose, but Voroshilovgrad brought wide recognition. Its mood recalls Jack Kerouac — the Ukrainian east rendered poetic, sometimes magical. After random events and strange coincidences the hero returns home to confront bandits and reclaim place.

He starts as a loser without family or tribe; by the end he has principles and knows he must fight for his past rather than abandon it.

Beyond books, Zhadan leads the rock band Zhadan and Dogs, hosts a podcast, writes political essays, composes opera librettos, and illustrated a poetry volume by artist Oleksandr Roitburd.

chapters.title: Engage youth, build cultural bridges
chapters.text:
While writers searched for new language, Ukrainian literature expanded rapidly in the 2000s and early 2010s, drawing younger readers back to domestic authors.

Yurii Vynnychuk, a veteran of historical and erotic fiction, revives the Austro-Hungarian myth of Lviv through local legend. Tango of Death follows four friends — Pole, Ukrainian, German, and Jew — whose paths diverge and reunite across World War II, holding friendship as the one constant amid catastrophe.

Andrii Kurkov remains Ukraine's best-known contemporary author abroad. Death and the Penguin, a surreal bestseller translated into thirty-plus languages, follows a journalist and his penguin through black-humor survival in post-communist Ukraine. Grey Bees (2018) explores the "gray zone" of the Russian-Ukrainian war, where two former classmates in an emptied village must reconcile, adapt, and protect their bees when politics empties the streets.

Kurkov practices cultural diplomacy: English PEN member since 1988, president of Ukrainian PEN from 2018, arguing that literature is foreign policy by other means.

In addition to writing, Andrii Kurkov is actively engaged in cultural diplomacy. Since 1988, he has been a member of the English PEN club, and in 2018 he became the president of the Ukrainian PEN.

Tania Maliarchuk, a surrealist prose master based in Vienna since 2011, writes for Deutsche Welle, continues in Ukrainian, and belongs to Ukrainian PEN. She won the Ingeborg Bachmann Award for Frösche im Meer (Frogs in the Sea), about generational gaps, and wrote A Biography of a Chance Miracle about a woman navigating western Ukraine's absurdities.

Sofia Andrukhovych offers a psychological counterpoint to her father Yurii's carnival prose. Felix Austria follows two friends in Stanislaviv (now Ivano-Frankivsk) at the century's turn as friendship slides into codependency, with Galician atmosphere and local food woven throughout every chapter.

Festivals like Lviv's Book Forum and Kyiv's Book Arsenal build reading communities; the Ukrainian Book Institute represents the country at international fairs.

Literary festivals play an important role in creating a reading community, particularly the largest — Book Forum in Lviv and Book Arsenal in Kyiv.

More than a hundred Ukrainian titles appear yearly in English, German, Polish, and other languages.

chapters.title: Afterword: light against darkness
chapters.text:
Ukraine's book market split into "before" and "after" 2014. The Revolution of Dignity began as a peaceful student protest in November 2013. By February 2014 Russia invaded Crimea; in April it moved on Luhansk and Donetsk. Culture policy shifted as the market distanced itself from Russian publishing and readers looked to domestic voices for meaning.

Writers have produced a vast shelf of war reflections — many now translated abroad. A sample:

• Mondegreen by Volodymyr Rafeienko traces a Donetsk writer's shift from Russian to Ukrainian, reconciling with an unfamiliar environment through language.
• In Isolation by Stanislav Aseyev recounts twenty-eight months in a Donetsk concentration camp, published after his release.
• Daughter by Tamara Duda, a volunteer's debut, follows a girl who loses everything in Donbas and becomes a fighter.
• How War Changed Rondo by Romana Romanyshyn and Andrii Lesiv (Agrafka Creative Association), an illustrated children's book on war that keeps hope intact, won international awards.
• Life Went on Anyway: Stories by Oleg Sentsov collects autobiographical pieces from the director held in Russian captivity 2014–2019, including a hunger strike for Ukrainian political prisoners.

Ukrainian writers have collected a considerable publishing portfolio of reflections on everything happening during the ninth year of the Russian-Ukrainian war. This list is just a fraction of it.

Although this trip was long, you've only seen the tip of the Ukrainian literary iceberg. Dive into modernist experiments, Ukrainian mythology, or philosophical prose — the journey through history and culture always offers more because you can always learn something new.

Try this

• Once you value your past, you invest in your future. Ukrainians have consistently chosen resistance over surrender. Find what anchors you, and courage follows.

Ukrainian Fiction 101

• Eneida by Ivan Kotliarevsky
• Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors by Mykhailo Kotsiubynskyi
• The Forest Song by Lesya Ukrainka
• The Hunters And The Hunted by Ivan Bahriany
• Voroshilovgrad by Serhii Zhadan
• Felix Austria by Sofia Andrukhovych

Read it till the end?
